title: Grasmere gingerbread
subtitle: Cumbrian gingerbread with oats, a crisp crumb, and strong ginger
excerpt: Grasmere gingerbread is not the soft, cakey gingerbread of most recipes. It is pressed into a tin as a crumbly oat-and-flour mixture, baked until set, and cut into squares while warm. It sits between a biscuit and a crumble: crisp when fresh, chewy when kept a day or two. Firmly spiced.
sourceNotes: Grasmere gingerbread is a uniquely Cumbrian biscuit associated with the village of Grasmere in the Lake District, where it has been sold from the same cottage by Sarah Nelson and her successors since the mid-nineteenth century. The original recipe is kept secret, but the texture and general character (an oat-based crumble pressing, firmly spiced with ginger) are well documented and widely reproduced in British regional baking literature.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool and freeze in a single layer. The texture holds well from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Grasmere gingerbread improves with 24 hours in a tin: the texture shifts from crumbly to chewy. It keeps well for up to 1 week.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the pressed slab bakes in 22 to 25 minutes until the surface is dry and set

glossaryTerms.definition: Grasmere gingerbread uses the same method as a crumble topping, but pressed into a tin and baked as a solid slab. The butter is rubbed into the dry ingredients to produce loose, crumbly crumbs, which are then pressed firmly into the tin. Pressing tightly is important: loose crumbs will not hold together after baking. The slab is cut into squares while still warm, before it crisps.

content.text: takes under 10 minutes to prepare. Use enough ginger to taste it clearly: this is not a politely flavoured biscuit. The oats give the texture; the golden syrup gives the slight chew.

Heat the oven to 160 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 20x20 cm square tin with baking paper. Combine 100 g plain flour, 100 g rolled oats, 100 g light brown sugar, 2 tsp ground ginger, 0.5 tsp mixed spice, and 0.25 tsp bicarbonate of soda. Rub in 100 g cold cubed butter until the mixture forms fine, even crumbs. Add 1 tbsp golden syrup and mix briefly; the crumbs will just start to clump. Press the mixture firmly and evenly into the prepared tin using the back of a spoon or the base of a glass. The surface should be compact and smooth.

Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the surface is set and dry and the edges have begun to colour. Remove from the oven and immediately mark into squares or rectangles with a sharp knife while still warm and soft. Leave to cool completely in the tin before lifting out on the paper. The gingerbread will firm as it cools.

items.solution: The mixture was not pressed firmly enough into the tin, or it was not cooled completely. Press as firmly as possible before baking and wait until fully cool before cutting or removing.

items.problem: Gingerbread is too hard when cool
items.solution: It overbaked. It should still feel slightly soft in the centre when it comes out of the oven. It firms considerably as it cools. Store in a tin for a day and the texture will soften slightly.
